coredata.dc:title: Sodium sulfite leads to the formation of fluorescent organic nanoparticles: Regulation from aqueous solution, cellular pathway to security design patterns
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Nengtao
Yu, Aiwen
Gao, Jinwei
Liu, Wanqiang
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) play important roles in the monitoring and controls of biological systems. To fulfill such duties, we have to investigate how the nanoplatforms interact with living matters. Currently, very limited information concerning the potential risks and toxicity has been reported for FONs except the MTT assay. In this contribution, the amine-rich cationic precursor (polyethyleneimine) initiates one-pot copolymerization with methyldopa sesquihydrate and water-soluble fluorescent nanoparticles are afforded. The influence of reduction agent (sodium sulfite) on the luminescence, microstructure and crystallinity has been discussed and the corresponding stabilities are substantially improved. Further studies reveal that selective Cu2+ detection has been realized due to “on-off” change and the limit of detection is determined to be 52 nM. It has been discovered that glutathione (GSH) can recover its green luminescence in terms of strong affinity of GSH to copper ions. For the first time, three different cell viability experiments including MTS assay, Annexin V-APC/7-AAD kit and double stain technique have been performed to support the negligible cytotoxicity of FONs in living cells. Spectra imaging has been employed for the identification of Cu2+ and GSH in HeLa cells. In addition, the Cu2+-GSH pairs can generate an “AND” logic gate during recognition sequence operations. It will be the first example that the effectiveness of encryption and decryption has been verified via Cu2+ and GSH based on FONs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Stability
Fluorescent organic nanoparticles
Cytotoxicity
Encryption

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and pH stability study
Cytotoxicity of S-PMDA NPs and cell apoptosis analysis
Detection of Cu2+ and GSH in aqueous solution
Determination of copper ions in laboratory tap water
Evaluation of biocompatibility and fluorescence imaging features in living cells
Logic gate operation of S-PMDA NPs towards Cu2+ and GSH

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Column graphs for the emissions of PMDA NPs and S-PMDA NPs in range of 0 h, 8 h, 16 h, 24 h. (Insets: Photographs PMDA NPs and S-PMDA NPs under UV light irradiation at 365 nm).
(a) TEM images of S-PMDA NPs (Inset: High-magnification image of S-PMDA NPs) and (b) DLS curve of S-PMDA NPs.

FT-IR spectra of Methyldopa (red), PMDA NPs (green) and S-PMDA NPs (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

UV–vis absorption (red), PL excitation (blue) and emission spectra (green) of S-PMDA NPs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) Emission spectra of S-PMDA NPs after addition of Cu2+ from 0 to 50 μM; (b) Linear plot of titration spectra. (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the S-PMDA NPs in the presence and absence of copper ions, respectively). (c) Emission spectra of S-PMDA NPs (0.2 mg/mL) and Cu2+ (15 μM) after the addition of GSH from 0 to 70 μM. (d) Linear plot of titration spectra (F and F0 are the fluorescence intensity of the S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ in the presence and absence of GSH, respectively).

Decay curves of S-PMDA NPs (red), S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ (green) and S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+/GSH (blue). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Typical percent distribution of HeLa obtained by Annexin V-APC/7-AAD staining experiments. Before measurement, cells of A was control and other cells were treated with various amounts of S-PMDA NPs (50 (B), 100 (C) and 225 μg/mL (D)).

Confocal microscopy images of HeLa cells treated with (a) S-PMDA NPs (0.2 mg/mL) (b) S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ and (c) S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+/GSH after 4 h. Note: (I) excited with 405 nm laser, (II)Bright field, (III) merged image of A and B. Scale bar = 20 μm.

Confocal fluorescence images of (a) HeLa cells incubated with S-PMDA NPs (0.2 m g/mL) after the addition of Cu2+ at different concentrations at 37 °C for 5 min and (b) HeLa cells incubated with S-PMDA/Cu2+ NPs (0.2 mg/mL/15 μM) after the addition of GSH with different concentrations at 37 °C for 5 min.
Schematic illustration of (a) information coding, (b) encryption and (c) decryption process by the S-PMDA NPs security ink under the 365 nm irradiation.
Schematic illustration of S-PMDA NPs synthesis and recognition process of Cu2+ and GSH.
Determination data of copper ions in tap water samples.

head.ce:title: Sodium sulfite leads to the formation of fluorescent organic nanoparticles: Regulation from aqueous solution, cellular pathway to security design patterns

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, 411201, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Hunan University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: . School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hunan University of Science and Technology, Xiangtan, 411201, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: The two authors contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) play important roles in the monitoring and controls of biological systems. To fulfill such duties, we have to investigate how the nanoplatforms interact with living matters. Currently, very limited information concerning the potential risks and toxicity has been reported for FONs except the MTT assay. In this contribution, the amine-rich cationic precursor (polyethyleneimine) initiates one-pot copolymerization with methyldopa sesquihydrate and water-soluble fluorescent nanoparticles are afforded. The influence of reduction agent (sodium sulfite) on the luminescence, microstructure and crystallinity has been discussed and the corresponding stabilities are substantially improved. Further studies reveal that selective Cu2+ detection has been realized due to “on-off” change and the limit of detection is determined to be 52 nM. It has been discovered that glutathione (GSH) can recover its green luminescence in terms of strong affinity of GSH to copper ions. For the first time, three different cell viability experiments including MTS assay, Annexin V-APC/7-AAD kit and double stain technique have been performed to support the negligible cytotoxicity of FONs in living cells. Spectra imaging has been employed for the identification of Cu2+ and GSH in HeLa cells. In addition, the Cu2+-GSH pairs can generate an “AND” logic gate during recognition sequence operations. It will be the first example that the effectiveness of encryption and decryption has been verified via Cu2+ and GSH based on FONs.

S-PMDA NPs-based nanodots were prepared via one-pot strategy from polyethyleneimine and methyldopa sesquihydrate. Its green luminescence could be responsive to Cu2+ and the quenching mechanism was discussed. The incorporated GSH firmly trapped copper ions and the green fluorescence was recovered. Their logic gate operations and security ink potentials were also assessed. Three different cell viability experiments including MTS assay, Annexin V-APC/7-AAD kit and double stain technique have been performed and no obvious cytotoxicity was observed.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscale entities including carbon dots, silver/gold nanoparticles, semiconductors, sulfur dots or graphene dots are most widely studied in several scientific disciplines such as physics, chemistry, engineering or computer sciences [1–5]. In recent years, the manipulations of structures at nanoscale levels are demonstrating promising potentials in biological or diagnostic analysis [6,7]. The small size provides many advantages since the nanosystems will interact with tissues within a molecular level with versatile functions. However, quite a few nanoparticles especially the noble or heavy metals have a negative impact on living matters and the cell survival rates are low [8]. Therefore, the evaluation of in vitro toxicity will be the primary step to develop nanodimension-based formulations for biomedical applications. Most of literatures concerning well-defined nanoparticles (for example, carbon dots) focus on the study of cell viability by using MTT assays. But the organic solvent employed in MTT method will cause protein precipitation in culture medium and lead to light scattering. In this research, the nanosystem-dependent cytotoxicity has been studied by three different approaches including MTS, Annexin V-APC/7-AAD apoptosis kit and double-stain technique. In addition, the absorbance curve in MTT assay is less sensitive than fluorescent dyes for recognizing viable cells. In this case, more accurate information to detect the cell viability has been achieved.

As for the sources with intrinsic emission properties, fluorescent organic nanoparticles (FONs) generate tremendous interests in diverse fields. The current developed cases would be mainly based on fluorescent inorganic nanoparticles. These granules were difficult to decompose and could cause toxicity because of accumulation in living systems. Therefore, the fluorescent organic nanoparticles have been preferred for the application in cellular imaging and diagnosis fields due to their low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, biodegradable feature and controllable functionality [7]. In particular, polydopamine and its derivatives, which bear multiple catechol, amine or imine moieties, can show affinity to amine thiol groups through Michael addition or Schiff base reactions [9]. The molecular structure containing quantities of NH or NH2 groups as donor-acceptor pairs in the nanodomains will induce visible emissions due to radiative recombinations [10]. Lately, a few publications reported the preparation of FONs via dopamine and cysteine or glutathione [11,12]. However, the mussel chemistry focuses on the synthesis of nanomaterials by polyethyleneimine and methyldopa sesquihydrate is very rare. Moreover, the novel indicator nanosystem based on dopamine analogues with very favorable bioavailability has never been studied.

The strong metal binding ability of catechol-based functional groups in FONs is closely related to a variety of applications including chemical sensing, medical applications and biocatalyst. In view of the analytes, the copper ion is one of the most significant trace transition metal that is closely related to water pollution and physiological regulations. In addition, glutathione (GSH), a significant antioxidant, is wide used in metabolic and biochemical reaction, and plays a vital role in protecting cellular components against reactive oxygen species and many physiological and pathological processes [13,14]. Although several routes concerning the detection of Cu2+ ions and GSH have been developed, most of them suffer from drawbacks of high limit of detection, complicated equipment, time-consuming synthesis and stability in biological buffers [15–17]. Hence, a quick and sensitive method for sequential monitoring Cu2+ ions and GSH in cell system is real a challenge.

In this work, the two precursors give rise to weak emissions and the assembly of polyethyleneimine and methyldopa sesquihydrate (PMDA NPs) at room temperature has induced striking green luminescence. After the incorporation of sodium sulfite, the achieved S-PMDA NPs gain more advantages over with PMDA NPs based on photostability and morphological controls. The emitted green lights can be employed for tracking Cu2+ ions and such switched off effect would be recovered in the presence of glutathione (Scheme 1 ). Cell interactions with S-PMDA NPs are strongly dependent on the intrinsic feature of nanosystems. Herein, three different ways (MTS, Annexin V-APC/7-AAD kit and double stain technique) for evaluating its biocompatibility have been performed and the results reveal that no apparent damage to biological cells has been observed. Futhermore, cells exposed to S-PMDA NPs in vitro have highlighted that distinct responses to Cu2+ and GSH at different concentrations are collected.

Methyldopa Sesquihydrate (C10H13NO4·1.5H2O, 98%), polyethyleneimine ((CH2CH2NH)n, M.W.600, 99%; M.W.1800, 99%; M.W.10,000, 99% and M.W.70,000, 50% aqueous solution), sodium sulfite (Na2SO3, AR, 98%), glutathione (C10H17N3O6S, 98%), cysteine (C6H8O6, 99%) and l-Arginine hydrochloride (C6H7NaO6, 99%) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. All the other chemicals were analytical reagents and used without further purification.

Fluorescence spectra were measured by using a Hitachi-4600 spectrophotometer with the xenon lamp as the light source (Japan). All the scan speed was fixed at 1500 nm/min. Both excitation and emission slit widths were fixed as 10.0 nm. UV–visible spectrophotometer was collected by a Shimadzu UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Japan). TEM images were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100HR transmission electron microscope. Nanoparticles size was also obtained with dynamic light scattering (DLS) using a particle size analyzer (Malvern Instrument, UK, Nano-ZS90). Decay curves were measured with a Transient/Stationary Fluorescence Spectrometer (Edinburgh FLS-920, UK). Zeiss confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM710) equipped with a laser at 405 nm was used to investigate the cellular images. MTS was measured at 490 nm using a Polarstar microplate reader, flow cytometric analysis was performed on the BD Accuri C6 Flow Cytometer.

The S-PMDA nanoparticles were synthesized by a facial one pot method. 10 μL of polyethyleneimine and 23.8 mg methyldopa sesquihydrate were added into 25 mL deionized water under magnetic stirring for 30 min at the room temperature. Then the color of solution turned from colorless to brown. In addition, when the excess sodium sulfite was added to the solution, the color changed to pale-yellow under magnetic stirring for 24 h. The product was collected by filtration and large particles were removed. The obtained solution was purified via the utilization of dialysis tube (molecular weight cutoff ~1000 Da) against the deionized water for 24 h. The S-PMDA NPs powder was obtained by freeze-dried treatment under vacuum for 30 h and the sample was kept for further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and pH stability study
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent spectra of S-PMDA NPs (0.2 mg/mL) were measured using fluorescence spectrometer with excitation wavelength of 385 nm. To evaluate stability of S-PMDA NPs under the different pH values, the fluorescence spectra was tested by adjusting the pH values (pH = 3 to pH = 12) of the reaction system. 0.2 mg/ml S-PMDA NPs at different pH values were prepared in aqueous solution. The pH values were measured by pH meter at room temperature and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

In the system of ions detection, the S-PMDA NPs were dispersed into 2 mL buffer solution to achieve the final concentration of 0.2 mg/mL. When copper ions at different concentrations were added into the above-mentioned solution, the fluorescence spectra were measured. With the other experimental conditions remained unchanged, a variety of ions (Na+, K+, Li+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, F−, Br−, Cl− and I−) were added in 0.4 mg/ml S-PMDA NPs solution and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

Human cervical cancer cell (HeLa cell) was used in this studies and HeLa cells were cultured in DMEM medium. The cells were supplemented with 10% heat-inactivated fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotics penicillin (PS) and maintained at 37 °C in a humidified condition of 95% air and 5% CO2 in culture medium for 24 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cytotoxicity of S-PMDA NPs and cell apoptosis analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The cell viability of S-PMDA NPs on HeLa cells was measured with MTS kits [18]. The HeLa cells were put into a 96-well microplate at a density of 5000 cells/well under the above conditions for 24 h. As for the control group, 8 wells were left with culture. After removal of the medium, HeLa cells were incubated with fresh medium containing S-PMDA NPs (0, 50, 100 and 225 μg/mL) for another 24 h. Then, 20 μL MTS solution was added to each well. At last, incubation for 2 h, the absorbance at 490 nm was collected by a microplate reader for further analysis.

To study cell apoptosis analysis of S-PMDA NPs via flow cytometry [19], HeLa cells were seeded in 12-well microtiter plates at a density of 5 × 105 cells/well under the above conditions for 24 h. Then fresh medium containing S-PMDA NPs (0, 50, 100 and 225 μg/mL) was used through replacing the original culture medium. Therefore the samples were incubated for another 4 h. After the cells were washed with binding buffer twice and centrifuged, another 100 μL binding buffer was added. Finally, 3 μL of Annexin V-APC and 7-AAD were added to each sample. Samples were incubated for 15 min and analyzed by flow cytometry.

The double stain technique for the identification of cell viabilities was presented as it follows: fluorescent dyes (PI [10 μg/mL] and Hoechst 33,258 [3 μg/mL]) were added to the above six-well plates with a density of 1.0 × 105 cells/well with medium containing S-PMDA NPs (0, 50, 100 and 225 μg/mL) for 1 h, which were incubated for 20 min. The cells were then observed by fluorescent microscope and the live/dead cells were differentiated (OLYMPUS IX70, Japan).

Briefly, HeLa cells were cultured in Lab-Tek chambered cover glass (4-well, Thermo Scientific, USA) with a density of 1 × 105 cells/well and grown to 70% confluency. After overnight incubation, the cells were treated with S-PMDA NPs at a final concentration of 200 μg/mL for 4 h at 37 °C and then washed three times with PBS buffer to remove excess S-PMDA NPs. As for the determination of copper ions in vitro, we have to mention an important point that all of the PBS buffer solutions have been replaced by HEPES buffer with pH = 6 because copper ions will be precipitated by PBS at alkaline condition. The as-cultured cells with S-PMDA NPs were further used for copper ions detection in vitro that the as-cultured cells were again washed three times with HEPES buffered saline (pH = 6) to remove the free S-PMDA NPs, and then incubated in HEPES buffered saline with 0, 5, 10, and 15 μM of copper ions for 1 h respectively. Then the cells were treated with 0, 5, 10, and 20 μM of GSH and washed three times with PBS. After that cell images were taken with a Zeiss confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM710) equipped with a laser at 405 nm.

The developed nanoscale materials with intrinsic fluorescence properties have generated extensive interests in numerous fields due to their surface groups. The coating modification of nanoparticles would be time-consuming since the synthesis process might involve several steps and need to be functionalized for each ligand. To circumvent such challenges, S-PMDA NPs were assembled through one-step stirring method by using methyldopa sesquihydrate and polyethylenimine (M.W.600) in water at room temperature. Under such mild conditions, the color of the mixture turned from colorless to brownish yellow in 30 min, suggesting the desired product was afforded. In the whole preparation, the time of this reaction will show dependence on the degree of polymerization and result in diverse emission features. The dopamine polymerization reaction would include consecutive incubation of quantum dots with methyldopa and amine-terminated precursor. Therefore, the optimized reaction time would be important. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at different time period of 5min, 10min, 20min, 30min, 40min and 50min (Fig. S1a). Based on the analysis, the emission intensities were rapidly improved depending on the time increase and the most suitable value was determined to be 30 min. We further explored the optimal experimental conditions for synthesising nanomaterials with different molecular weight of PEI (PEI600, PEI1800, PEI10000, PEI70000) under the same circumstances. The emission curves were given in Fig. S1b and the results supported that the combination of methyldopa and PEI600 was the best choice. It has been reported that the reduction treatment for the graphene quantum dots would lead to more defects and such defect states would interfere with recombination of electron-hole pairs. Then the quantum yield could be enhanced [20]. In this work, sodium sulfite was used as the reduction agent to control the surface chemistry of the poly-dopamine particles. In this way, the experiments were performed in the presence (S-PMDA NPs) or absence of sodium sulfite (PMDA NPs) and the fluorescence spectra were measured during 0 h, 8 h, 16 h and 24 h respectively (Fig. 1 ). Upon the extension of the time duration, the emission intensity was substantially reduced for the sample of PMDA NPs. However, S-PMDA NPs exhibited no apparent changes after 24 h, indicating that the generated electro-donating groups would maintain high emission efficiency.

The surface modification strategy has been explored and their microscopic information was recorded. The transmission electron microscopy images (TEM) of S-PMDA NPs and PMDA NPs were collected and compared. In Fig. 2 a, the nanocrystals possessed nearly spherical shapes and exhibited good monodispersity with an average particle size of 7 nm in the range from 6 to 12 nm. The granules were easily dispersed in water without aggregation. Moreover, the hydrodynamic size of S-PMDA NPs was about 7.5 nm, which was plotted in Fig. 2b via dynamic light scattering and the curve revealed a narrow distribution in the range between 5 and 11 nm. As described in Figs. S2a–b, there were no significant difference for the particle size between S-PMDA NPs and PMDA NPs after the preparation immediately. 24 h later, the detailed morphological studies revealed the S-PMDA NPs could keep the diameter of the particles unchanged. But shape evolution effect was obtained for PMDA NPs and the average size would reach as large as 30 nm. The addition of sodium sulfite was considered not only as the reduction agent, but also as the stabilizing reagent and the serious aggregation was suppressed. In this case, the green luminescence of S-PMDA NPs was restored as described in Fig. 1.

The crystalline structure of the S-PMDA NPs was represented by the inplane lattice spacing of 0.210 nm, which was corresponding to the (103) diffraction plane of diamond-like (sp3) carbon [21,22] (Fig. 2a). However, the PMDA NPs displayed an amorphous scattering image of diffuse rings in Fig. S3 and a well-defined reflection for the crystalline diffraction was found in S-PMDA NPs (Fig. S3b), showing S-PMDA NPs had a higher degree of crystallization after sodium sulfite treatment. Compared with XRD pattern of the PMDA NPs (Fig. S4), the sharp reflection peak at about 24° (2θ) of S-PMDA NPs was detected, which might be attributed to carbon atoms with the lattice spacing of 002 facet [23,24].

To further clarify the chemical compositions within the material, the PMDA NPs and S-PMDA NPs were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and zeta potentials. As provided in Fig. 3 , the characteristic peak at 1695 cm−1 for CO–NH appeared in the curve of PMDA NPs and such amide vibration mode I was absent from pure methyldopa (Fig. 3, red line). Due to the incorporation of sodium sulfite, the peaks of S-PMDA NPs at 1695 cm−1 and 1596 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and the bending vibration of N–H. The peak at 3400 cm−1 were assigned to O–H stretching vibrations and N–H stretching vibrations. It is worth saying that the –OH and amide vibration modes I/II exhibited significantly higher intensity than PMDA NPs, indicating the ester or carboxyl groups were transformed into hydroxyl or CONH units. In addition, the zeta potential values were presented in Fig. S6. The mean surface charge of PMDA NPs was determined to be −6.86 mV and the surface charge of S-PMDA NPs was increased to −2.06 mV, which may be ascribed to the reduction of high amounts of carboxyl groups after reaction with Na2SO3.

The photophysical processes and energy of transition via electron movement were discussed by UV–vis absorption spectra and FL excitation/emission spectra (Fig. 4 ). Two absorbance bands at 290 nm and 360 nm were found in the absorption curve. The short band at 290 nm was assigned to the π–π* transitions corresponding to conjugating planes and the signal at 360 nm was ascribed to the n-π* transition of carbonyl/amine functional groups on the surface [25–27]. In the same graph, the bright green luminescence of the S-PMDA NPs was observed at 535 nm when excited by 385 nm. The Stokes shift was measured to be 150 nm and the quantum yield could reach 5.8% [28,29]. The related fluorescent nanoparticles and literatures were summarized in Table S1. Although the quantum yield in this contribution was not the highest, still it gave an acceptable value for the luminescent output. It has to be mentioned that all the preparation procedures were performed at room temperature and no external energy would be necessary. Moreover, the treatment of sodium sulfite substantially improved its photostability.

In particular, the emission spectra were scanned with the excitation wavelength increasing from 320 to 420 nm in Fig. S7. The peak wavelengths were scarcely shifted with the changes of excitation wavelength. These findings may support the fact of particles with narrow size distributions in Fig. 2 and it may also indicate the uniform chemical features on the surfaces. To investigate the pH effect in the aqueous solution, the fluorescence of S-PMDA NPs were examined between pH range from 3.0 to 12.0. The nanoparticle was stable and its emission spectra (Fig. S8) varied insignificantly under a wide pH range from 3.0 to 12.0, which would be favorable for further sensing and cellular image purposes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cu2+ and GSH in aqueous solution
ce:section.ce:para: Responsive fluorescence for the determination of chemical or biochemical analytes is an active area of scientific research. The most direct sensing approach is when the intensity of emissive species changes according to the guests. In the presence of Cu2+, the evolution profiles of S-PMDA NPs was evaluated under the titration experiments. Cu2+ ions in the range of 0–50 μM were dripped into the aqueous solution of S-PMDA NPs and the fluorescence intensity at 535 nm of was gradually reduced, which was given in Fig. 5 a. Fig. 5b presented the relationship between the quenching value (F0–F) and the concentration of Cu2+ ranged from 0 μM to 50 μM, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensity of S-PMDA NPs in the absence and presence of Cu2+. A good linear relationship (y = 19.19x+45.86) was achieved between the fluorescent quenching value (y) and the concentration of Cu2+ (x) with R2 = 0.9965. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 15 nM based on 3SD/k (LOD = 3SD/k, where SD is the standard error of 10 blank experiments and k is the slope of the calibration curve). Compared to numerous reported methods [30,31], the current way not only possessed efficient preparation processes with easy operation but also exhibited excellent sensitivity. To exploit the response time of the fluorescent probe, the emission change of S-PMDA NPs in the presence of 20 μM Cu2+ was recorded in Fig. S9b. From the above results, the optical signal was quickly switched off within only 3 s upon the addition of Cu2+, indicating the fast-response feature toward Cu2+ detection was realized.

In addition to the high sensitivity and fast responses, selectivity is a primary factor that governs the sensing process in a complex medium. Therefore, the influences of several interferences from other common ions (Mg2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, K+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−) on the Cu2+ detection were performed in Fig. S9a. It was found that none of these ions had significant influence on the fluorescence intensity of S-PMDA NPs, which verified the excellent selectivity toward Cu2+ was obtained.

Although Cu2+ caused the drastic change in the emission curves, the possibility to develop the “on-off-on” sequential probe should be explored. As provided in Fig. S10, the green fluorescence at 535 nm could be generated again with the addition of 70 μM GSH. It has been reported that Cu2+ easily formed stable complexes with GSH [32,33] and Cu2+ may be released from the S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ aggregates, which induced the recovery of the emission. The above results supported the feasibility of S-PMDA NPs for the application of dual targets sensing. Upon the addition of GSH, the quenched fluorescence of S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ was recovered immediately within 10 s in Fig. S9c. Moreover, the recovered fluorescence intensity remained almost constant during the incubation time from 10s to 10 min, indicating that it can be potentially used as a sensing probe in competitive environments. To study the high sensitivity, the spectral files were recorded with the addition of GSH at different concentrations in the range of 0–70 μM (Fig. 5c). Based on the collected data, the fluorescence intensity difference (y, (y = F–F0)) was linear with the GSH concentration (x) in the range of 0–70 μM (F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensities of S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ system in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively). After fitting calculation, the linear regression equation was expressed as y = 10.40x-41.31 with R2 = 0.9917 in Fig. 5d. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated as 28 nM for GSH (LOD = 3SD/k, where SD: the standard error of 10 blank experiments and k: the slope of the calibration curve). In order to exclude other interferences for sensing system, the effects of related amino acids (Arg and Cys) and other ions (Cr2O7 2−, CrO4 −, Br−, Ac−, F−, I−, NO2 −, NO3 −, PO4 3−, S2−, SO3 2− and SO4 2−) on the emission intensity were monitored in Fig. S9d. None of these interferents caused apparent changes in photoluminescence. All these results confirmed that the nano-platform owned high sensitivity, rapid response and well selectivity for GSH detection.

To elucidate the quenching mechanism of Cu2+ sensing system, the decay lifetimes were measured, which can be assigned to either dynamic or static quenching process. The decay lifetimes (τ) of S-PMDA NPs in the absence and presence of copper were given in Fig. 6 . Based on a monoexponential fit, the decay lifetimes (τ) were determined to be 5.94 ns and 5.88 ns, revealing the two values were virtually almost overlapped. This phenomenon suggested that static quenching played an important role in the interaction of Cu2+ ion with S-PMDA NPs. To further search for the evidence for the quenching process, the changes in the microstructure of S-PMDA NPs were studied. As shown in Fig. S11a, the well-dispersed S-PMDA NPs were regular in spherical shape. After the incorporation of copper ions, a substantial increase in the particle diameter was observed and the aggregation-caused-quenching was occurred (Fig. S11b). Due to the addition of GSH, Cu2+ may be trapped from the S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ aggregates and the fluorescence can be restored. In fact, the decay lifetimes of S-PMDA NPs upon the sequential addition of Cu2+ and GSH were collected in Fig. 6 and no particular changes were monitored. The titration of GSH possessed the ability to control the morphology and the underlying process was derived from the coordination between GSH and copper ions. This is why we observed that the considerable tiny particles were formed again in Fig. S11c. In terms of the strong affinity of GSH to Cu2+, the extent of active components of conjugation was prohibited and the formation of excimers avoided.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of copper ions in laboratory tap water
ce:section.ce:para: We also made great efforts to employ such nanoplatform in the real water detection. Copper ions in tap water were analyzed by recovery experiments. As described in Table 1 , different concentrations of Cu2+ in tap water could be precisely determined. More importantly, the recovery rates of Cu2+ was ranged from 99.00% to 101.50%, which can to be acceptable in real sample detection. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was less than 3%, suggesting that the probe was very suitable for the quantitative detection of Cu2+ in real environment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of biocompatibility and fluorescence imaging features in living cells
ce:section.ce:para: For any optical sensor, it is essential to demonstrate its capability in biological analysis. Cytotoxicity and biocompatibility of S-PMDA NPs were investigated via incubation with HeLa cells by MTS assays. As provided in Fig. S12, the cell viability was above 91% upon exposure to a variety of S-PMDA NPs at different concentrations (0, 50, 100 and 225 μg/mL) after 24 h incubation, it can be seen that S-PMDA NPs have negligible toxicity in living cells. While most of reported literatures only used the above MTS assay kit for the determination of cell growth, with the aim of proving its reliability, here also Annexin V-APC/7-AAD kit was utilized to analyze the cell apoptosis by detecting phosphatidyl serine expression and membrane permeability. To our knowledge, it is the first time to provide the separation between apoptotic and live cells based on polydopamine-type organic nanoparticles. In Fig. 7 , the results suggested that no specific change can be observed in living cells treated with 0, 50, 100 and 225 μg/mL of S-PMDA NPs at 24 h. Even at higher concentration (225 μg/mL), the late apoptotic or dead cells (Q2 region) was still less than 5%, the performance firmly supported that S-PMDA NPs had excellent biocompatibility. In this contribution, an interesting double-stain technique was developed to assist the qualitative analysis of cell viabilities via eye visualization. Two commercial dyes (Hoechst 33,258 and Propidium Iodide) were utilized to culture HeLa cells and emission wavelengths of the dyes were distinct from S-PMDA NPs. Hoechst 33,258 which emits blue fluorescence can penetrate into both living and dead cells. As for Propidium Iodide (PI dye), it can only enter damaged or dead cells and emit red fluorescence. Based on the method, we can quickly and efficiently identify cell viabilities through eye observation in the presence of S-PMDA NPs (Fig. S13). At the same range of concentrations (0, 50, 100 and 225 μg/ml S-PMDA NPs), very limited dead cells with red color were found. The above cytotoxicity profiles indicated that the nanoprobe can be safely used as a noninvasive cellular imaging agent.

Cell staining is important for a wide range of diagnosis processes and the crucial matter in nanotoxicological effects has been ruled out in previous studies. After HeLa cells were pretreated with S-PMDA NPs (0.2 mg/mL) at 37 °C for 4 h, intensive green fluorescence was observed by using confocal laser scanning microscopy in Fig. 8 a. The investigation of interaction between S-PMDA NPs and Cu2+ in vitro provides an efficient and accessible way to explore cellular responses to specific metal ions. In Fig. 8b, the green luminescence was severely inhibited with the addition of 15 μM Cu2+. Furthermore, the presence of 20 μM of GSH can capture changes with recovery of fluorescence and such strategy can accurately to recognize Cu2+ and GSH in a sequential order. These results clearly showed that S-PMDA NPs not only can be employed for imaging purposes, but also possess the potentials for real-time monitoring of two analytes. To further exploit whether the nanoplatform could be applied to quantify Cu2+ or GSH, Cu2+ solution at different concentrations (0, 5, 10 and 15 μM) were incorporated into the S-PMDA NPs pretreated HeLa cells. As demonstrated in Fig. 9 a, the green emission of S-PMDA NPs was progressively reduced with the increase of the Cu2+ ions and the results were consistent with the photoluminescence studies. After GSH at different concentrations (0, 5, 10 and 20 μM) were introduced into the S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ pretreated cells, the bright green luminescence was gradually switched on. Such evaluation experiments indicated that the nanoprobe could be utilized for semiquantitative detection of Cu2+ and GSH in living cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Logic gate operation of S-PMDA NPs towards Cu2+ and GSH
ce:section.ce:para: In recognition system, logic gates have been explored to clarify the detection processes. Due to the unique fluorescence recovery behavior of the S-PMDA NPs/Cu2+ system in the presence of GSH, a suitable “AND” logic gate was developed by using 50 μM Cu2+ and 70 μM GSH as the inputs, and the fluorescence signal recovery was considered as the output. For the inputs, the states of “1” and “0” were assigned to the presence and absence of the two chemical inputs Cu2+ and GSH, respectively. For the output, fluorescence recovery and non-fluorescence recovery intensity were regarded as “1”and“0”, respectively. In the absence of input (0/0) and Cu2+(1/0) or GSH (0/1) input alone, no fluorescence recovery was observed, the result was equivalent to“0”. In contrast, only the system was stimulated by two inputs together (1/1), the fluorescence signal could be generated and an output signal of the gate was “1”. Therefore, a molecular logic gate was demonstrated where the truth Table (a) and symbol (b) were illustrated in Fig. S14.

The nanoplatform was assessed as an imaging tool for security information detection and its role as solid-state luminescent material was established. On the basis of the above findings, a secure encrypted strategy was developed by using the water-soluble S-PMDA NPs solution as functional ink for information coding and encryption as given in Fig. S15. During the first step of treatment, the Chinese characters were written on filter paper via a brush pen. The pictures of paper were observed under daylight condition and 365 nm UV light irradiation (Figs. S15a and S15b). It was clear that the Chinese characters were hidden at daylight irradiation. However, the bright green signal of the Chinese characters was observed under ultra-violet excitations, indicating that the S-PMDA NPs ink could be efficiently employed for encoding encryption.

Until recently, a variety of carbon dots has been developed for sensing purposes or imaging orientation [34–36]. But the utilization of polydopamine dots as patterning agents or security ink in the presence of analytes has been very limited up to now, at the best of our knowledge. Here, after the English word “security” was recorded on filter paper (Fig. 10 a), the information was encrypted by using nontoxic and easily available CuCl2 solution and the process was similar to the recognition of Cu2+ in fluorometric titrations. After CuCl2 solution was sprayed onto the paper, the fluorescence of S-PMDA NPs was switched off. The patterns of the characters on the filter paper were almost negligible under both 365 nm UV light (Fig. 10b), indicating the coded information was encrypted by CuCl2. To make the process to be recycled, GSH solution was incorporated into the paper, the green fluorescent English word “security” was recovered again under UV light illumination (Fig. 10c), showing that the encrypted information was translated. In this way, information coding, encryption and decryption can be fulfilled by employing the S-PMDA NPs solution as fluorescent security ink. CuCl2 and GSH could be used as the encryption and decryption reagents to convey highly confidential messages.

In summary, we reported the synthesis and biological application of a S-PMDA NPs-based nanoplatform via one-pot strategy from polyethyleneimine and methyldopa sesquihydrate. The corresponding stabilities were significantly improved due to the reduction treatment of sodium sulfite. Its green luminescence could be turned off in the presence of Cu2+ and the quenching mechanism was discussed. The incorporated GSH firmly trapped copper ions and the green fluorescence was recovered. Their logic gate operations and security ink potentials were also assessed. Determination of the biocompatibility of nanosystems will be highly valuable for the biological evaluations. In current research, three different cell viability experiments including MTS assay, Annexin V-APC/7-AAD kit and double stain technique have been performed, and no obvious cytotoxicity was observed. The sequential recognition via fluorescence intensity change demonstrated a good correlation with the analytes concentration during in vitro examinations. These results revealed that the polydopamine nanosystem with intrinsic emissions would allow one to monitor the two targets in aqueous solution and evaluate the biological responses effectively, which will be essential to future environmental studies or medical diagnosis.

Nengtao Wu: Data curation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Aiwen Yu: Writing - review & editing. Jinwei Gao: Writing - original draft. Wanqiang Liu: Software, Resources. Qianming Wang: Formal analysis, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: J. W. thanks grants from National Natural Science Foundation of China-Guangdong Joint funding support (No. U1801256) and Innovation team project by the Department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009). Q. M. appreciates National Natural Science Foundation of China (No 21371063).
